observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. The Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly follow the guidelines provided in the submission of the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) and attach the necessary supporting documents to support the reconciling items enumerated in the BRS; b. The Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to send written notices to the payees one month before the checks become stale. Stale checks shall be reported and attached to the RCI prepared at the period of cancellation; c. The accountant shall make the necessary adjusting entries for stale-dated checks as follows; i. For stale checks over 6 months but not over 2 year: DR: Cash in Bank-LCCA Pxxx recognized in the books. CR: Accounts payable Pxxx ii. For checks over 2 year: DR: Cash in Bank – LCCA Pxxx CR: Prior Years Adjustment* Pxxx (*to represent surplus adjustment)
observations.managementAction: a) Not Implemented b) Not Implemented c) Per latest BRSs of the LGU, identified reconciling items are still forwarded thus, are not adjusted
observations.reason: Updated BRSs are not prepared and submitted as of year-end. Stale checks are still carried forward thus, are not adjusted in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to: a. Require the Inventory Team to conduct complete annual physical inventory-taking of PPE by type and submit a Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) not later than January 31 of each year and reconcile the RPCPPE with the ledgers balances; b. Require the GSO-Designate to maintain property cards and reconciled with the ledger cards of the Municipal Accounting Office; c. Require the GSO/MTO to determine to whom the missing properties were issued. He shall then issue a demand for the production, otherwise the accountable officer should be asked to apply for relief of accountability following prescribed procedures/requirements; and d. Direct the Municipal Accountant to: (a) recognize depreciation expense monthly on all the PPE accounts; (b) make necessary adjustments in books to correct unrecognized deprecation of identified four PPE accounts and make necessary disclosure as to the four PPE accounts that are fully depreciated but still in use; and (c) adopt all the principles prescribed in IPSAS 17 for proper accounting of the Municipality’s PPE and for a fair presentation the asset accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: a) Not Implemented b) Not Implemented c) Not Implemented d) Not Implemented
observations.reason: RPCPPE not submitted and implemented actions to reconcile PPEs with ledger balances not yet completed; depreciation not recognized on a monthly basis; unserviceable/missing PPEs still not disposed or written-off.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to transfer and record the unexpended balance of the QRF and DRRMF to the Special Trust Fund under the Trust Liability-DRRM account in the Trust Fund book. Moreover, prepare adjusting entries to correct the errors in recording of related transactions. The Municipal Accountant should also be reminded to follow the prescribed format (Annex D of COA Circular 2012-002) to discuss the unexpended balance in the Notes to Financial Statement.
observations.reason: The unexpended balances were not transferred to Special Trust Fund; required subsidiary ledgers not maintained; notes disclosures not prepared as prescribed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all the Revenue Collection Clerks to remit to the Municipal Treasurer the full amount of their collections on a daily basis regardless of the nature of collections and that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact all her collections, as well as all collection turned over to her by the collectors/tellers, daily or not later than the next banking day. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately stop the practice of the immediate use of the funding for operations and that the Municipal Mayor should require that casual employees should not perform collection functions unless duly designated and bonded.
observations.reason: Collections continued to be deposited late; casual collectors still performing collections; accumulation of undeposited collections remained as noted in CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require both the BAC Chairman and Secretariat, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to: a. For each procurement, submit notarized Omnibus Sworn Statement of suppliers and written recommendations from End-user or any unit authorized by the Head of Procuring/Project Award; b. For each supplier, submit Mayor’s Permit or Business Permit and Income Tax Returns in accordance to GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020; and c. Henceforth, submit all the documentary requirements in the GPPB Circular for succeeding transactions.
observations.reason: Some required procurement documents (notarized OSS, supporting documentary requirements) were still not consistently submitted for COVID-19 procurements during CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Require the Members of the Sangguniang Bayan to enact a supplemental budget for any changes in the AIP and Annual Budget to cover new PPAs from realigned funds or available funds from new revenue source as certified by the Municipal Treasurer; b. Require the members of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to prepare a supplemental AIP to support the changes in the Annual AIP; and c. Direct the Municipal Budget Officer and Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to revise the LDRRMFIP and include new programmed appropriations and obligations for disaster risk reduction and management programs, projects & activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years which were transferred to the Special Trust Fund.
observations.reason: Supplemental AIP and necessary budgetary approvals for the re-aligned PPAs were not prepared/approved; LDRRMFIP revision not fully implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended strict adherence to Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer exercise due diligence in the processing of claims and ensure complete documentation before effecting payment. The Accountant should submit the lacking documents for evaluation.
observations.reason: Supporting documents for infrastructure payments were not fully submitted as of audit; deficiencies persisted in CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Monitor the implementation of the programs, projects and activities (PPA); b. Coordinate with the BAC on the prompt scheduling of procurements activities of PPAs included in the LDRRMFIP; c. Coordinate with the Municipal Accountant in the preparation of the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to ensure that disbursements are in accordance with the approved LDRRMFIP; and d. Submit the required Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the COA for onward submission to the Audit Team on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.
observations.reason: Monthly reports either not prepared or not submitted timely; LDRRMFIP monitoring and procurement scheduling coordination not fully implemented; utilization remained low (63.49% reported for CY2021).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE: a) cause the creation of a Disposal Committee to take charge of the disposal of unserviceable assets b) require the Supply Officer to submit to the Disposal Committee the Inventory and Inspection of Unserviceable Property and Waste Material Report.
observations.reason: Unserviceable properties not yet disposed; Disposal Committee not created; inventory and inspection reports not submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive consider creating and organizing an Internal Audit Services, in compliance with R.A. 4177 and AO 70 to give assurance for a sound and adequate internal control system in the operations of the Municipality.
observations.reason: IAS not yet organized; municipality has not designated an Internal Audit head or established IAS functions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended strict adherence to Sec. 4(6) of PD 1445 and that claims be paid only upon submission of complete documents. Said measure must be carried out to prevent suspension or disallowance during post-audit.
observations.reason: Supporting documents remained not submitted; payments still processed without full documentation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor gives utmost
importance on presenting in the financial statements PPE account balances that
arevalidand reliableby requiring theInventoryCommittee andtheGSOto:

a. Conduct a complete physical inventory of all the Municipality’s assets and
prepare and submit the required report thereon. Investigate any
discrepancies between the inventory report and the accounting and property
records and make the necessary adjustments where the error was committed;

b. Maintain complete and updated property cards, as a counter check to the
correctnessof theaccounting records;

c. Prepareandmaintain PAR withcompletedetails; and

d. Prepare and submit the approved Physical Inventory Plan to the Audit Team
at least 10 days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities in
adopting COA Circular 2020-006 relative to the one-time cleansing of PPE
accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to
submit to the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the
name of the taxpayers and amount due and collectible at the start of each year to
serve as basis in setting-up the Real Property Tax/Special Education tax
Receivables in order to reflect a reliable RPT and SET Receivables accounts
balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
verify with the Provincial Accountant the discrepancies of P586,029.27 and
P88,464.28 between the recorded balances of the reciprocal accounts reflected in
the books of the Province and adjust the same to present the correct balances of
thesaidaccountsintheMunicipality’sfinancial statement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Social Welfare
and Development Officer (MSWDO) to coordinate with the DSWD Region IV-A on
theguidelinesand properutilizationof thefunds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MPDO to incorporate in
the Annual Investment Plan of the Municipality the unutilized funds from the PCA
Municipal Share in cutting permit fees and utilize the same in compliance with
Section2of RA No. 10593.

Werecommendedthat theMunicipal Mayor:
a.Require the Bids and Award Committee (BAC) to strictly observe the
regulationsset forthunder RA No. 9184 intheprocurement of goods; and

b.Stopthe practiceof purchasingfuelinbulk.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the preparation and
submission of Daily Gas Issue Record, Monthly Summary of RIV, Monthly Report
of Official Travel for each vehicle and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and
ensure that Driver’s Trip Tickets are serially numbered and all information
necessaryareindicatedfor every tripundertakenfor proper control.

1.19 We recommendedthat the General Services Officer and Accountant update
and reconcile their records, and avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE
accounts per COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

1.20 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly comply with the
recognition criteria for PPE and prepare a journal entry voucher (JEV) to:
(a) Adjust the incorrect recognition of PPE amounting P1,100,000.00 duly
supported with the original JEV and the JEVs of the recipient barangays on the
asset recognition, and
(b) Recognize depreciation on PPEs duly supported with a schedule of depreciated
properties.

2.16 We recommended that:
a) The Municipal Treasurer submit at year-end the list of unreleased checks
for all funds to the Municipal Accountant; and
b) The Municipal Accountant to:
i. regularly and timely prepare bank reconciliation statements for all
bank accounts.
ii. make the necessary JEVs to recognize the unrecorded bank accounts,
adjust identified book reconciling items, and cancel stale checks
provided that the foregoing is properly supported withappropriate
documentation.
iii. Restore unreleased checks by preparing a journal entry voucher at the
end of the year based on the list submitted from the MTO. At the start
of the ensuing year, draw another entry to reverse the previous entry
made and recognize the availability of the checks for release.

3.7 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace to prior years’ accounting
entries to ascertain the cause of the negative balances and effect the necessary
adjustments, and/or the Municipal Treasurer demand refund of overpayment where
necessary.

4.14 We recommended that the Municipal Social and Development Officer together with
the Property/Supply Officer:
a. Conduct physical count of all the remaining donated items.
b. Prepare inventory list of the remaining donated items for DRRM every month or
every quarter or until all donations are fully distributed.
c. Henceforth, prepare all the reports and documents required to account and monitor
the receipt and issuances of all donations in kind and submit a copy of all the reports
to the Audit Team.

4.15 We recommended that the Municipal Property Officer furnish the Municipal
Accountant with the duplicate copies of all Acknowledgment Receipts of in-kind
donations, invoice receipt and inventory list of donated items.

4.16 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare a journal entry
voucher to reclassify donations in cash from Other Payables to Trust
Liabilities–DRRM. Also, summarize data from the documents submitted by the
Municipal Social and Development Officer and Property Officer, compare
records with the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM maintained by the
Municipal Social and Development Officer for check and balance, and disclose the
necessary information in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

5.15 We recommended that:
a) the Municipal Accountant, Budget Officer and MDRRMO reconcile
their records to determine the correct available balance of the
LDDRMF. And revert to the general fund the prior years QRF and
MOOE unexpended balances that are beyond five years.
b) The Budget Officer to maintain the RAAO for LDRRM Funds.
c) The Accountant trace the prior years entries of transactions charged
against the Special Trust Fund and prepare an adjusting entry to
reflect those transactions in the Trust Liabilities-DRRM account; and
prepare a subsidiary ledger of the Trust Liabilities-DRRM account by
year of transfer/ by LGU/ by donor, as applicable.

6.18 We recommended:
a) the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator to specify the projects to be undertaken from the
appropriations and to prepare a revised and/or supplemental AIP to
support changes in the projects before they are implemented.
b) the Budget Officer and MPDC coordinate in the monitoring of utilization
and implementation of PAPs under the 20% Development Fund to prevent
overutilization, and the latter to request augmentation of funds, where
necessary.

6.19 We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to recognize in the books the
loan amortization on the New LGU Building for the 1st semester 2021
amounting to P506,602.55.

6.20 It was further recommended during the exit conference that a Supplemented AIP
be executed for the PAPs in Table 19.

7.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor approve the Purchase Orders on all
future transactions of the Municipality, and through the Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC), require requesting office/s to indicate all necessary
specifications of items for purchase. We also recommend the Accountant to
refrain from processing payments that are not supported with the proper
documentation.

8.11 In order to establish the regularity of the grant of the AHDP/SRA, we
recommend that the Rural Health Unit personnel and other concerned offices
to submit lacking documents.

8.12 We also recommended the Municipal Accountant ensure all necessary
supporting documents are attached to claims before processing payments.
Henceforth, only disbursement vouchers/payrolls with complete supporting
documents should be processed for payment. This is without prejudice to the
issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

9.8 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Revenue Collection
Clerks to remit their collections daily before 5:00pm or before leaving the office in
case of emergency leave to the Municipal Treasurer. Also, direct the Municipal
Treasurer to deposit intact all her collection, as well as collections turned over to
her by the Revenue Collecting Clerks, daily or not later than the next banking day.

10.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
i. the refund of unexpended fund transfers to their respective source
agencies, and immediate reversion of the P40,000.00 Countrywide
Development Fund (CDF) to the Bureau of Treasury. The CDF was
declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court in the case of Belgica
vs. Ochoa (G.R. No. 208566, November 19, 2013, En Banc).
ii. the Accountant retrieve prior years’ records as proof of double
payment on DSWD-Supplemental Feeding, and require the supplier to
refund the overpaid amount,
iii. the Accountant prepare the aging of fund transfers and provide
disclosures on the complete composition and dormant balances of Due
to NGA in the Notes to Financial Statements, and
iv. reconcile with the source agency and request for write-off the dormant
fund transfers, if warranted, in accordance with COA Circular No.
2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to faithfully carry out objectives by which the BGCM Fund has been created and adhere to the guidelines on the use of the said fund to avoid inappropriate charging and misuse of funds.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant justified the charging of tires for the government vehicles which is strictly due to an emergency situation during the COVID-19 outbreak. No BGCM Fund was received in CY2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to immediately present for audit the lacking documents to substantiate the aforementioned BGCM transactions and practice more efficiency and economy in succeeding transactions despite its urgency in order to address the COVID-19 emergency in the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Lacking documents were already submitted on June 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MDRRMO to submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances together with the required supporting documents to the NDRRMC through the OCD, copy furnish the Audit Team within 10 working days after the end of each quarter pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-009 in order for the Audit Team and the oversight agencies to conduct timely evaluation of the receipt and utilization of the aids and donations received by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: List was already submitted on May 2021. There were no donations received from other agency or private citizen for CY 2021

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to submit to the Municipal Accountant a duly Certified List of Taxpayers showing the name of the taxpayers and amount due and collectibles at the start of each year to serve as basis in setting-up the RPT/SET Receivables in order to reflect a reliable RPT and SET Receivable accountsbalances.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor stated that the Assessment Roll was submitted to the Municipal Treasurer on March 25, 2022 and mentioned that they were not able to furnish the copy of the Assessment Roll before the end of December 2021 due to some revision/re-assessment/correction done on the file. They assured that they will continue with the practice of submitting the monthly transactions with the Municipal Treasurer to keep them updated on the changes in the Assessment Roll.
observations.reason: This observation was already noted in Annual Audit Reports (AAR) for CYs 2019 - 2020. However, there was still no set-up made by the Municipal Accountant at the start of the year because the Certified List of Taxpayers was not submitted at the beginning of CY 2022 due to the late submission of the Assessment Roll.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor gives utmost importance on presenting in the financial statements PPE account balances that are valid and reliable by requiring the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory of all the Municipality’s assets and prepare and submit the required report thereon. Investigate any discrepancies between the inventory report and the accounting and property records and make the necessary adjustments where the error was committed; and Municipal Treasurer to maintain complete and updated property cards, as a counter check to the correctness of the accounting records.
observations.managementAction: The Management’s conduct of physical count is still on-going. However, the Municipality still fails to conduct a complete physical count as can be seen on the submitted RPCPPE.
observations.reason: This observation was already noted in Annual Audit Reports (AAR) for Cys 2012-2020. Reiterated in Part II, Observation No. 1, Page No. 39

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer/GSO-Designate to institute measures to properly completely dispose of all fully-depreciated and unserviceable properties in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and Sections 379 to 381 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality initiated the disposal of the unserviceable properties but did not push through because no willing buyer was qualified to the criteria set by the GSO. The Designated GSO wanted to reconcile first the PPE before disposing the said properties.
observations.reason: In CY 2021, the Municipality initiated the disposal of the unserviceable properties but did not push through because no willing buyer was qualified to the criteria set by the GSO.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the immediate titling of subject lots in the name of the Municipality and continuously coordinate with other government agencies to avoid contingent claims that may arise in the future in compliance with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386.
observations.managementAction: The titling of land is still on-going.
observations.reason: The Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Treasurer was not able to transfer any of the remaining untitled lots in the name of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Market Administrator and the Municipal Treasurer to implement measures to improve the financial performance of the public market. If the identified strategies are still not viable, consider the possibility of converting the vacant/unoccupied space of the building to other economic enterprise or other purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee and the Municipal Accountant to make representation with any of the contractor to provide any of the securities mentioned in the IRR of RA No. 9184 for the above-mentioned advance payments. We also recommended to ensure that all subsequent advance payments are supported with the required security.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant deducted from the contractor's first billing on March and May 2021.
observations.reason: No payment for mobilization was incurred in CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the Municipal Engineer to:  conduct detailed engineering activities prior to the design and bidding of the project so as to avoid issues that could delay the implementation of the PPAs; and  explain/justify why no liquidated damages were imposed against the Contractor for late completion of the Construction/Improvement/Rehabilitation of various municipal streets.
observations.managementAction: Detailed engineering activities prior to design and bidding was being conducted by the Municipal Engineer. The Municipal Engineer has already submitted an explanation letter including the schedule of time elapse and the actual survey of the road on May 19, 2021, justifying the non imposition of the liquidated damages to the contractor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MPDO and the Municipal Engineer to: • submit the list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year to the Office of the Auditor at the beginning of the year; • inform the Auditors within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices were already posted for validation; • submit the monthly monitoring report as current as possible and updated; and • ensure the inclusion of all necessary information on project signboards in compliance with the above-cited circular.
observations.managementAction: List was already submitted at the beginning of the year. The Municipal Engineer informed the Auditors within 10 days after the award of the appropriate project contract. The Municipal Engineer submit regularly the Monthly monitoring report. The Municipality has already complied with the inclusion of all necessary information on project signboards.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor directs the MGFP and the MPDO in coordination with the different offices, the full establishment of the GAD Database that will assist the gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation of the Municipality to ensure that the GAD programs and activities implemented are responsive to the distinct gender issues of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: GAD Database was already established by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Person: a. together with the Heads of the Implementing Units, to ensure that all programs, plans and activities are properly planned and that problems that may arise be considered in planning the PPAs to be implemented; and b. to regularly observe/monitor the effective implementation and utilization of GAD Funds for the benefit of its intended beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person ensured that all PPAs are properly planned. The Municipality has implemented 65 percent accomplishment of GAD PPAs despite the presence of COVID -19 pandemic

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO to maximize the use of available resources on the LDRRMF for disaster mitigation and preparedness in order to build the resilience of the Municipality against disasters.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO utilized the DRRM Fund.
observations.reason: Of the P3,599,728.23 utilized the DRRM Mitigation Fund, only 55 percent were utilized. Furthermore, Continuing Appropriation from CY 2019 for the Construction of Evacuation Center, was still not implemented in CY2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all the Revenue Collection Clerks to remit to the Municipal Treasurer the full amount of their collections on a daily basis regardless of the nature of collections and that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact all her collections, as well as all collections turned over to her by the collectors/tellers, daily or not later than the next banking day.
observations.managementAction: a) Implemented b) Not Implemented c) Not Implemented
observations.reason: Updated BRSs are not prepared and submitted as of year-end; there are still collectors who are casual employees performing collection functions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Strictly require each Department Head to comply with the monitoring and reporting of inventories issued and prepare the RSMI on a monthly basis and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant; b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to draw a JV to base on RSMIs and record inventory expenses on a monthly basis; c. Constitute an Inventory Committee which shall conduct the physical count of inventories at least once a year, prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories and submit the same to the Audit Team not later than January 31 each year; d. Require the GSO-Designate to submit the RIS for procured items costing P4,520,072.50. Henceforth all issuance of supplies should be done by the GSO Designate and no longer by the Municipal Treasurer; e. Direct the MDRRMO to submit the list of recipients of rice distributed complete with details on quantity received and signatures of the recipients. Submit also the distribution list for Barangay Tangkaan and Barangay Lungsodaan for evaluation; f. Direct the Municipal Accountant to draw an adjusting entry to record unused medical supplies costing P176,420.00; and g. Require the RHU to submit to GSO-Designate and Municipal Accountant the Monthly Report of Inventory of Medicines for Distribution for reconciliation of records.
observations.managementAction: a) Implemented b) Not Implemented c) Not Implemented d) Implemented e) Not Implemented f) Not Implemented g) Not Implemented
observations.reason: RIS are signed by the GSO Designate and issuance procedures adjusted; some required reports (e.g., RSMIs, distribution lists, monthly reports) not submitted or prepared; adjustments for unused medical supplies not yet recorded.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor cause the implementation of a Payroll System to distribute salaries via Automated Teller Machines (ATMs). We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor limit cash advances granted to the Disbursing Officers within the limit of their maximum cash accountability or increased their fidelity bond sufficient to cover their maximum cash accountability. Also, the Disbursing Officer should liquidate cash advances for salaries and wages within five (5) days from after each fifteen/end of month pay period.
observations.reason: The Municipality started paying salaries via ATM on May 2021; other controls regarding cash advances were to be enforced.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the Local Sanggunian to enact a supplemental budget to cover new PPAs funded by new revenue source or additional fund received; b. Direct the Local Development Council to prepare and update its Annual Investment Program or Supplemental AIP to cover new PPAs in the Supplemental Budget; c. Direct the BAC to post the updated APP, NOA and other information relative to the Procurement Projects undertaken out of the BGCM funds; and d. Henceforth, submit all documentary requirements in the GPPB Circular for succeeding transactions.
observations.reason: Supplemental PPAs were enacted and APP/NOA and related procurement documentation were posted; supplemental budget executed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit the paid payrolls for meal subsidies complete with signatures by all payees. Also, the Municipal Treasurer should make sure that paid payrolls should be signed and received by all payees; b. Direct the in-charge of the Municipal quarantine facility to submit the list of returning OFW and LSI who were isolated in the facility. The list must include details on the actual time of their arrival in the facility; and c. Direct the RHU to submit the DTRs of front liners on duty in the quarantine facility and submit to COA for validation of payments of cash subsidies for the front liners.
observations.reason: Paid payrolls and supporting documents were submitted (e.g., on Sep. 2, 2021), and DTRs were provided for validation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Cause the creation of policies and guidelines to address the issue on the necessity and propriety of hiring JO workers taking into consideration the existing regular personnel; b. Limit the hiring of Job Order workers to those extremely necessary and within the age limit, and deploy only to offices that actually are in need of their services; c. Require the Human Resource Officer to require Job Order workers to submit primary personal identification, undergo interview and evaluate the performance of each to give assurance that those hired have the skills required by the office; d. Require the Human Resource Officer to incorporate in the contract the number of man hours, quantity and duration of the work to be performed by the JO worker and require them to submit quantified accomplishment reports to support payment of wages; and e. Cause the reduction of number of job-order workers to accommodate the increase of daily rate pursuant to minimum wage required under Wage Order No. RB VIII-21.
observations.reason: HR measures instituted: policies/guidelines on JO hiring implemented; JO workers limited to essential roles; interviews and documentation required; however, daily rate adjustments for JO not fully aligned with Wage Order (some JO daily rate noted at P315 which is below required P325).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the HRMO to adhere to provision of Section 77 of RA No. 7160 which provides that the Municipality may hire Job-order for local projects only, authorized by the Sangguniang Bayan.
observations.reason: Job Orders deemed necessary by the Municipality were designated for local projects and an IAS/HR controls instituted to prevent duplication.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to submit to the Municipal Accountant a duly Certified List of Taxpayers showing complete detail of the amount due and collectibles at the start of each year to serve as basis in setting-up the RPT/SET Receivables in order to reflect a reliable RPT and SET Receivable accountsbalances.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer failed to submit a duly Certified List of Taxpayers.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
faithfully carry out objectives by which the BGCM Fund has been created and

chargingandmisuse of funds.

immediately present for audit the lacking documents to substantiate
aforementioned BGCM transactions and practice more efficiency and economy in

emergencyintheMunicipality.

the taxpayers and amount due and collectibles at the start of each year to serve

a.Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory of all the

Investigate any discrepancies between the inventory report and the accounting

DR: Cash in Bank-LCCA P xxx

2020; and

Management Council to revise the Local Disaster Risk Reduction
Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) and embodied therewith new
programmed appropriations and obligations for disaster risk reduction and
management program, project & activities to be funded from the unexpended

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor gives utmost importance on presenting in the financial statements PPE account balances that are valid and reliable by requiring the:

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Treasurer/GSO-Designate to institute measures to properly and completely dispose all fully-depreciated and unserviceable properties in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and Sections 379 to 381 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer appraised values of the unserviceable properties posted in the IIRUP.
observations.reason: The Municipality was preparing for disposal in early CY 2020, but due to the pandemic, the disposal of the fully-depreciated and unserviceable propertieswerenot facilitated.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the immediate titling of subject lots in the name of the Municipality and continuously coordinate with other government agencies to avoid contingent claims that may arise in the future in compliance with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386.
We further recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer include in the appropriated budget appropriation the funds for the titling of the said lots.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor stated that the Municipality has continuously exerting effort to immediately facilitate the titling of the subject lots. She mentioned that they have coordinated with the LRA and as per verification with the said agency, the Municipality found out that the lot located in Barangay Burgos was identified as lot no. 1716 and was registered in the Ordinary Land Registration proceeding under GLRO Record No. 29639 with a Decree No. 336394 issued on July 14,1928, previously named “Tayabas” as its location. She also stated that she requested funds for the research of these Municipal Lots in the Supplemental Budget of the Municipality. Likewise, she proposed a relocation survey for the Municipal property in support of the requirements and the immediate titling of the said lots.
observations.reason: Land remains untitled since further verification of the ownership of the land is still on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to confirm with the account owners/suppliers and to request them to submit to the creditors documents pertaining to their claims. If they failed to do so within a reasonable period of time after they were notified, revert the accounts to the Unappropriated Surplus of the GF, subject to an authority from the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) through a resolution, with a condition that appropriation shall be made in the event that claims arises in the future pursuant to the provisions of Section 98 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality refunded the said Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable refunded the creditors and informed various creditors for the claims/refund of creditors.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Market Administrator and the Municipal Treasurer to formulate strategies and implement measures to improve the financial performance of the public market. If the identified strategies are still not viable, consider the possibility of converting the vacant/unoccupied space of the building to other economic enterprise or otherpurposes.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Market Administrator stated in his letter dated June 3, 2021 that the Market Committee is currently evaluating the application of new stallholders. Once completed, additional stallholders will occupy the public market that will increase the collections of the Municipality
observations.reason: The Public Market operation still incurred loss and not yet self-liquidating.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned municipal officials to immediately take action since the funds were remain already available for more than three years, with the end of providing immediate and adequate social and livelihood services projects for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality refunded the said amount to the Bureau of Treasury under Check No. 3077050 dated June 4, 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to utilize the unexpended balance of funds allocated funds for the intended purposes after due evaluation whether these are still priority concerns taking into consideration current developments and to accordingly realign the funds of projects/program/activity which have become irrelevant or not responsive to current priority undertakings.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality implemented projects under 20 percent DF and reprogrammed the projects which were no longer relevant.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor initiates, through the MGFP and the MPDC, the establishment of a GAD database that will assist the gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation of the Municipality to ensure that the GAD programs and activities implemented are responsive to the distinct gender issues of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person distributed questionnaires to the concerned agencies as initial measure in Reiterated in Audit Observation no. 12, page56
observations.reason: The GAD database was still insufficient since only 35 percent of the data werecollected.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Person:
a.together with the Heads of the Implementing Units, to ensure that all programs, plans and activities (PPAs) are properly planned and that problems that may arise be considered in planning the PPAs to be implemented; and
b.to regularly observe/monitor the effective implementation and utilization of GAD Funds for the benefit of its intended beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal GAD Focal Person, through her letter dated May 31, 2021, agreed to comply with the recommendation and ensure that all PPAs for the year 2021 are properly monitored, and that problems that may arise are considered in planning the PPAs for the effective implementation and utilization of GAD Funds.
observations.reason: Due to the COVID-19 pandemic in CY 2020, effective implementation and utilization of the GAD funds were not properly monitored.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the LDRRMO to maximize the use of available resources on the LDRRMF for disaster mitigation and preparedness in order to build the resilience of the Municipality against disasters.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO informed the Audit Team through his letter dated May 19, 2021, that the Construction of the Operation Center was not implemented since the supplemental budget amounting to P2,500,000.00 that was supposed to be an additional allotment for the said project was re-programmed to respond to the national health emergency due to COVID-19 pandemic, but he averred that fund was allotted this year, subject for approval by the Local Development Council. He also stated that purchase of rescue vehicle amounting to P2,200,000.00 was implemented in the first half of CY 2021, and that the remaining balance is still for implementation in the succeeding months. He assured that they will do their best to maximize the use of available resources of the LDRRMF for disaster preparedness and disaster prevention and mitigation and strictly implement the LDRRMFIP in the following years to come.
observations.reason: Some plan, projects and activities were still not implemented.

observations.recommendation:

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor prioritize the establishment of the LDRRMO in compliance with RA No. 10121 to ensure the safety and protection of the Municipality’s constituents during the times of disaster and emergency.

Municipality’s assets and prepare and submit the required report thereon.

dispose all fully-depreciated and unserviceable properties in accordance with

and collectible at the start of each year to serve as basis in setting-up the Real

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
confirm with the account owners/suppliers and to request them to submit
documents pertaining to their claims. If they failed to do so within a reasonable
period of time after they were notified, revert the accounts to the Unappropriated
Surplus of the GF, subject to an authority from the Sangguniang Bayan (SB)

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Market
Administrator and the Municipal Treasurer to formulate strategies and implement
measures to improve the financial performance of the public market. If the

vacant/unoccupied space of the building to other economic enterprise or other

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant, General
Services Officer and Municipal Engineer to strictly follow the accounting and reporting
procedures mentioned in COA Circular No. 2015-008 and COA Circular No. 2016-004 for
their guidance in making necessary journal entries to record back to Property, Plant and
Equipment all Public Infrastructures that was transferred to Registry of Public Infrastructure so that
fair presentation of the financial statements could be attained.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
a. require the inventory committee to include in its annual inventory taking
the road network account and reconcile the RCPPE with the records of
the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant;
b. require the Municipal Accountant to determine the cost of the road lot and
recognize it as part of the road network component including the cost of
constructing the drainage and slope protection and other miscellaneous
structure, if there is any;
c. require the Municipal Accountant to periodically assess the local roads
whether there is any indication that a component of the local road may be
impaired and recognize an Impairment Loss to be presented as a contra-
asset account;
d. cause the creation of an Inventory Committee and require the Committee
50
to conduct an inventory of all the local roads under its jurisdiction, prepare
and submit a verified Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network
System (RPCRNS); and
e. require the Municipal Accountant to maintain and update subsidiary
ledgers. and disclose in the notes to financial statements the local road
network system as prescribed by regulation.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the General Services
Officer to institute measures for the immediate and proper disposal of all
obsolete and unserviceable properties stipulated in Section 370 of RA No.
7160. Also, once proper disposal has been undertaken, the Municipal
Accountant should derecognize in the books said unserviceable PPE items.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to adopt monitoring mechanism to detect variances in the amount to be remitted from the
salaries withheld for the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and Philhealth contributions/loan payments of
employees. Furthermore, we also recommend that accounting records such as cash
receipts journal, check disbursement journal, cash disbursement journal, general and
subsidiary ledgers must be updated monthly in order to determine the exact amount to be
remitted to the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and Philhealth.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to properly classify the current portion of the Loans Payable-Domestic at year-
end in compliance with IPSAS 1 and provide sufficient appropriate disclosure
on the Loans Payable-Domestic account in the Notes to Financial Statements
to provide sufficient and adequate information so that the liquidity and solvency
of the municipality could be properly assessed

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
a. require the BAC to give utmost priority to the bidding process and shall be
on a “jury duty” until the Notice of Award is issued by the HOPE in order to
complete the entire procurement process at the earliest possible time; and
b. require the Municipal Budget Officer to make sure that all programs and
activities that are to be implemented by the Municipality under the 20% DF
should be covered with an appropriation ordinance and approved Annual
Investment Plan and make sure that 20% DF shall be available exclusively
for the specific purpose for which they have been appropriated.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Disaster
Risk Reduction and Management Officer to:
a. monitor the implementation of the programs, projects and activities (PPA);
b. coordinate with the BAC on the prompt scheduling of procurements
activities of PPAs included in the LDRRMFIP;
c. coordinate with the Municipal Accountant in the preparation of the Monthly
Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to ensure that disbursements are
in accordance with the approved LDRRMFIP; and
d. submit the required Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF
to the Municipal Accountant for onward submission on or before the 15th
day after end of each month to the Audit Team pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2012-002.
64
We likewise recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to formulate programs,
projects and activities related to DRRM corresponding to the available funds
and incorporate in the LDRRMFIP to maximize utilization thereof.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Engineer, Municipal Planning and Development
Officer, and the Municipal Agriculturist:
a. to disburse funds strictly in accordance with the work and financial plan
timelines, otherwise, a revised and approved disbursement schedule shall
be submitted;
b. to fast track the disbursement process while ensuring complete and
adequate supporting documents to facilitate submission of liquidation to the
Audit Team; and
c. to make sure that the proposed line item budget of the project funded by
fund transfer is in concurrence with the current market price.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
a. cause the creation of policies and guidelines to address the issue on the
necessity and propriety of hiring JO workers taking into consideration the
existing regular employees;
b. limit the hiring of personnel under Job Order contracts to those extremely
necessary and within the age limit, and deploy only to offices that actually
are in need of their services;
c. require the Human Resource Officer to require Job Order workers to submit
primary personal identification, undergo interview and evaluate the
performance of each to give assurance that those hired have the skills
required by the office; and
d. require the Human Resource Officer to incorporate in the contract the
number of man hours, quantity and duration of the work to be performed by
the JO worker and require them to submit quantified accomplishment
reports to support payment of wages.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
a. require the Local Finance Committee to adhere to Paragraph (a) and (b)
of Section 316 of the Local Government Code of 1991 which requires that
budget should be lodged in the income reasonably projected and collectible
for the ensuing fiscal year and that appropriately support the budget;
b. require the Municipal Treasurer to submit Status Report on the Income
from Waterworks System Fees; and
c. coordinate with the members of the Sangguniang Bayan to look into the
possible updating of the Ordinances on Water Rates based on the report
of the Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
a. require the Municipal Treasurer, In-charge of the Public Market Operation
and other members of the Local Finance Committee to review the
provisions in the Contract of Lease and make the necessary revisions
thereon especially on penalties of delinquent lessees; and
b. execute Water Service Contracts to its concessionaires stipulating the
terms, conditions and penalties in case of non-payments.
Secondly, we recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal
Treasurer:
a. issue demand letters to delinquent market lessees; and
b. issue notices to vacate for failure to pay the rent after the issuance of
demand letters.
Lastly, we recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal
Treasurer to:
a. continue to monitor the monthly collections of the billed water
consumption for the Office and take proper action on non-paying
consumers; and
b. issue demand letters to delinquent water service concessionaires and
exercise the right to disconnect service lines for reasons of failure to
comply with the demand letter within the stipulated period.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the GAD Focal Person
to conduct gender analysis in the formulation of programs and projects and
activities so that the intended beneficiaries are assured of the services,
protection and other assistance that may be provided.

Furthermore, we recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the GAD
Focal Person to exert all efforts to fast-track the implementation of
programmed activities in the Gender and Development Plan and Budget or
reprogrammed those GAD activities whose implementation was funded by
National Government Agencies.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant:
a. to identify the payees/accountable officers of all unsettled disallowances;
b. to issue demand letter with the signature of the Mayor to the
payees/accountable officers concerned with unsettled disallowances;
c. to enforce withholding of payment or any amount due to the persons liable
to settle said disallowances; and
d. to ensure that all employees who are retiring or transferring to other
agencies shall first settle the disallowances and charges for which they are
liable in accordance with Section 7 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated
September 15, 2009.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Health
Officer to facilitate the licensing of its ambulance with the DOH and secure
the transfer documents of the donated vehicle from the DOH.

We further recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal
Accountant to record in its books the cost of the donated vehicle supported
with the transfer documents from the DOH.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor and the Local Sanggunian to:
a. craft ordinances and issue executive orders related to waste management
and environmental protection;
b. strengthen the current waste management system of the Municipality,
including, garbage classification, collection, and recycling;
c. add more waste recycling facilities; and

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough analysis to determine the depreciation of the Municipal building and derecognize the carrying value of the demolished building; and b) direct the property officer to prepare a Waste Materials Report to record the remnants salvaged from the said demolition.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to facilitate the preparation of the program of works and fast-track the implementation of programs, projects and activities that are included in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) for 20% DF to attain socio-economic development and environmental goals of the municipality. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and to exercise due diligence in the processing of claims. We further recommend that before effecting payment complete documentations must be observed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer and Disaster Risk Reduction Management Focal Person to formulate a Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) for programs/projects/activities related to disaster management and prevention. Also, direct that LDRRMFIP will include the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of previous years which were transferred to the Special Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC Chairman, Municipal Engineer, MPDC, Municipal Agricultural Officer and Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer to judiciously plan proposed projects and fast-track the implementation of programs, projects and activities funded by the National Government Agencies in accordance with the timeline provided for in the MOA.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to: a. ensure that all conditions preliminary to the commencement of construction have been addressed and that a certification stating be executed by the Municipal Engineer prior to the start of procurement activities to ensure that necessary arrangements and documents have been done for the project to be completed and utilized as planned; b. review thoroughly the request for time extension by contractors based on complete and relevant documents that will enable the assessment of the reasonableness of the reasons cited; c. specify in the notice of suspension of work the period within which work is suspended and the reason for granting the request to facilitate monitoring; d. ensure the timely issuance of notice of resumption of work, so work is started by the contractors immediately after the period of work suspension; and e. maintain complete and orderly files of documents pertaining to project implementation such as approved contract time extensions, notices of suspension of work and resumption of work to facilitate review and monitoring, and to ensure that appropriate action are acted on time. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor: f. direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) (a) to properly plan the procurement of infrastructure projects; (b) to initiate the forfeiture of Performance Security of erring contractors in favor of the Municipality in case of non-completion of projects within the contract period; and (c) to initiate the sanction of blacklisting erring contractors, if warranted. g. require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to see to it that liquidated damages will be effected on every day of delay until the project is completed and accepted by the Municipality; We also recommended that the BAC must plan to ensure their completion and use within the planned period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the members of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to rescind the contract and to impose appropriate sanctions on top of the liquidated damages to be paid for contracts whose liquidated damages exceeded the 10% ceiling per RIRR of RA No. 9184 to address the continuing delay in the delivery of goods.

Considering the LGU would continuously secure loans and borrowings to finance development and investment projects, we recommended the following measures to address these deficiencies: - The maximum borrowing capacity should not consider the 20% debt service but also the net available funds prior to contracting of loans/borrowings; - The LGU through the LCE should conduct self-assessment of their capacity to repay the loan taking into consideration their net available funds, ensure that utilization of loan proceeds is covered by appropriation ordinance, determine the most cost effective borrowing scheme, contract loan only upon proper evaluation of priority projects, and allocate sufficient funds to cover total contracted liabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer to execute contract of lease to the lessees of the public market. The contract must stipulate the terms, conditions and penalties in case of non-payments. Also, the corresponding rental fee should be based on the approved revenue code of the Municipality. The Treasury Office must establish a system of collection to enforce collection of market stall rental fee periodically and demand for the collection of the unpaid amount.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the SWM Officer to consider the appropriate means of communication depending on the information that it wishes to pass on to the public and its stakeholders. For instance, there should be public notices on television and on the radio about who can access the Residual Containment Area (RCA), the working hours at the RCA to limit after-hours access and the schedule for collection of wastes from households for the different zones. Websites have become increasingly effective and economical in disseminating information. Therefore it is important to have a specific link for Solid Waste Management within the Municipality’s website so that it is easier for the public to have access to information and policy on solid waste management as well as any issue concerning solid waste management. To ensure effective implementation of the solid waste management plan, a well-designed, current and targeted community awareness program is required. The targeted audience should be considered when determining how the information should be given to the public. The urgency of the matter should also be considered so that everyone is aware of the matter as soon as possible.

We recommended that the RCA be enforced at the source, and that the wastes be placed at the site, which will provide baseline data to assist in developing effective waste minimization strategies and future plans for waste management. The SWM Focal Person must ensure that waste records are maintained to monitor the volumes and types of waste disposed.
We recommended that the SWM Focal Person should implement a color coding or appropriate means of identification to ensure that wastes are properly segregated at the RCA.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the SWM Focal Person to maintain disposal site records in the RCA.

We recommended that the RCA be fenced with a secured gate at the entrance to minimize illegal visitors or scavengers. This will also help address the occupational health and safety issues by limiting access.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) cause the allocation of funds to purchase the appropriate safety clothing as prescribed in the Manual for Operation and Maintenance at the Landfill; b) Direct the SWM Focal Person to ensure that any person entering the RCA must wear the proper safety clothing so that the operations at the RCA are effective and safe.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to see to it that gender-related programs, projects and activities are aligned with GAD-related undertakings so that the intended beneficiaries are assured of the services, protection and other assistance that may be provided.
